coredata.dc:title: Effect of toluene-4-sulfonic acid monohydrate concentrations on properties of polyaniline for pyrene detection via photoluminescence spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Beygisangchin, Mahnoush
Rashid, Suraya Abdul
Lim, Hong Ngee
Shafie, Suhaidi
Sadrolhosseini, Amir Reza
coredata.dc:description: Detection of pyrene as a toxic chemical is vital to possess a healthy, non-polluted and well sustainable environment. Toluene-4-sulfonic acid monohydrate (PTSA) doped Polyaniline in emeraldine salt (PANI) as a suitable material can be used to develop a new sensing material for pyrene detection. The relative amounts of PANI and PTSA are still an important aspect to realize the optimum combination. In this study, PANI and different concentrations of PTSA doped PANI were prepared using chemical polymerization of the monomer's aniline in acid environments at room temperature. UV–visible spectroscopy (UV–vis), Fourier transform infrared light (FT-IR), field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), electrical analysis, and photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL) were applied to determine bandgap values, physical and structural properties, morphology patterns, electrical conductivity, and fluorescence intensity respectively. The high conductivity value was obtained in 1% PTSA doped PANI with the value of 27 × 10−1 (Ω cm)−1 due to decreasing the bandgap value. Also, 1% PTSA doped PANI demonstrates high PL intensity owing to its orderly organized benzenoid and quinoid parts. Particularly, 1% PTSA doped PANI was selected as a suitable candidate for pyrene detection with a linear range of 0.001–10 ppm and a limit of detection of 7 × 10−3 ppm. This work provides a facile method to prepare a novel sensing material for the detection of pyrene with high sensitivity and fast response.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Detection of pyrene as a toxic chemical is vital to possess a healthy, non-polluted and well sustainable environment. Toluene-4-sulfonic acid monohydrate (PTSA) doped Polyaniline in emeraldine salt (PANI) as a suitable material can be used to develop a new sensing material for pyrene detection. The relative amounts of PANI and PTSA are still an important aspect to realize the optimum combination. In this study, PANI and different concentrations of PTSA doped PANI were prepared using chemical polymerization of the monomer's aniline in acid environments at room temperature. UV–visible spectroscopy (UV–vis), Fourier transform infrared light (FT-IR), field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), electrical analysis, and photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL) were applied to determine bandgap values, physical and structural properties, morphology patterns, electrical conductivity, and fluorescence intensity respectively. The high conductivity value was obtained in 1% PTSA doped PANI with the value of 27 × 10−1 (Ω cm)−1 due to decreasing the bandgap value. Also, 1% PTSA doped PANI demonstrates high PL intensity owing to its orderly organized benzenoid and quinoid parts. Particularly, 1% PTSA doped PANI was selected as a suitable candidate for pyrene detection with a linear range of 0.001–10 ppm and a limit of detection of 7 × 10−3 ppm. This work provides a facile method to prepare a novel sensing material for the detection of pyrene with high sensitivity and fast response.

ce:section.ce:para: Pyrene is a toxic chemical that is assumed priority owing to its hazard to human natural surroundings and the environment. For a non-polluted, healthy, and well ecological environment, there is a requirement for a novel technique that is accomplished of detecting and quantifying pyrene and constant monitoring [1,2]. Numerous methods have been established for the detection of pyrene, like gas chromatography (GC), high-performance liquid chromatography, mass spectrometry (MS), Fourier Transform Infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), and Raman spectroscopy [3,4]. Even though these techniques are selective, sensitive and provide trustworthy measurement, most of them are expensive and need a long time of samples preparation, large desktop apparatus, eligible operators, insufficient diagnosis for limits of detection and require a bulky volume of samples for extraction and separation.

A novel technique of quantification, detection and constant monitoring of pyrene, is biotic to preserving a non-contaminated, healthy, and ecological atmosphere. Technical development in sensors is determined using worries about the health and safety of civilization [5]. Sensor procedures are commonly performed by metal oxides operational at maximum temperatures. Therefore, novel and unique materials that can dominate the limits of detection of metal oxides will be discovered.

Electrically conductive polymers (CPs) are a kind of multifunctional material that may be used in various applications spanning a broad range of scientific disciplines, including chemical and biological research and physics, material science, and engineering. Since their identification by Shirakawa, MacDiarmid, and Heeger et al. [6] in 1977, CPs have received much interest from investigators who want to learn more about the behaviour and characteristics of conductive polymers. CPs can be synthesized and prepared via two available routes, i.e., the electrochemical and chemical methods [7]. Moreover, CPs possess exciting features such as unique electrical, thermophysical, easy synthesis and low cost to use in various commercial applications such as sensors, electronic displays, supercapacitors, transistor and memory devices, etc. [8]. Between the different conductive polymers like polypyrrole (PPy), polythiophene (PT), polyaniline (PANI), polyparaphenylene (PPP), and polyphenylvinylenes (PPV), PANI is a type of semi-flexible polymer that has special electrical and optical properties [3,8].

PANI is a unique conducting polymer that consists of enormous units of aniline monomers through a chemical formula of C6H5NH2, and it can produce a long chain of PANI using chemical or electrochemical methods [8,9]. On the other hand, PANI is a tremendous and favourable polymer that possesses easy synthesis, low cost, flexible control of its electrical properties by both charge transmission protonation and doping, and environmental stability [10]. Due to its extraordinary properties, PANI possesses possible usages in several fields, like solar cells [11], optoelectronic devices [12,13], electroluminescence technologies [14], supercapacitors [15], sensors [16], biosensor [17], scaffolds [18], delivery systems [19], neural prosthesis/biotic–abiotic interfaces [20], gas separation membranes [21], anti-corrosion material [22], and photovoltaic cells [23]. PANI was first discovered in 1862 by Letheby et al. [24] and is originate in three ideal oxidation conditions through the aniline's polymerization: (a) leucoemeraldine (white/clear), (b) emeraldine (salt-green/base-blue), and (c) pernigraniline (blue/violet) (Fig. 1 ). Pernigraniline base is the term used to describe completely oxidized PANI. Like that of the emerald base, semi-oxidized PANI is decreased, while PANI as the lecomeraldine base is lowered entirely [8,25].

Nonetheless, the conjugated polymer's conductivity is moderately low since the covalently bonded polymers have no valence band to oblige as a way for electrons to transfer nearby. Typically, a doping procedure is completed to increase the electron arrangements in an orbital. This is done using the process of oxidation (p-doping) or reduction (n-doping). Additionally, the electrical conductivity of PANI can be improved using doping concentrations [26,27]. The dopant holds charge carters that arrange for vacant spaces to be completed by the electrons. Consequently, charges flow occurs, and the charges can be moved more simply and conduct electricity [28,29]. The impacts of the doping level in electricity conduction are stronger than the thermal conductivity of PANI, therefore significantly influencing the proportion of thermal effectiveness [30].

Zhang et al. [31] prepared PANI with worm-like organizations. They established the further structures of PANI with the maximum conductivity using the high HCl concentration [31]. They likewise presented that the rise in conductivity could be owing to the maximum density of electrons and the reduction in the PANI size. These outcomes were also approved by the other examiners, as the PANI's morphology touches the conductivity of π-conjugation polymers because of the density of electrons and the organization of PANI molecular chains [32]. PANI's flow of electrons, which traverses along the worm-like nanorods and further extends to the branching knobbles, perfectly illustrates this phenomenon. The movement leads to increased charge transfer, so it is useful for increasing electrical conductivity [33]. Although dopant has a significant role in improving the conductivity of PANI, the high dopant concentrations similarly lead to a reduction in the conductivity [31]. A similar result was achieved by Rahayu et al. [34], who found that the PANI moisture using different HCl concentrations improved the conductivity before the addition of subsequent concentrations decreased the conductivity value. This was caused by the damage to the polymer chain structures in high acid concentrations [34].

PANI is applicable to arrest pyrene that is surrounded by a carboxyl functional group and aromatic rings [3,35]. PANI has tremendous chemical and physical features such as environmental stability, high conductivity, corrosion resistance and homogenous morphology. PANI has functional groups such as sulfonated poly (aniline-co-o-aminophenol) and s-copolymer which can be synthesized by chemical polymerization procedures and doped with toluene-4-sulfonic acid monohydrate (PTSA) concentrations for improving its optical and electrical properties [36]. Therefore, PTSA doped PANI in the best PTSA concentration is the proper material for the detection of pyrene. Examining the interface of PTSA doped PANI in the best PTSA concentration with pyrene can serve to develop the vital application and understanding of PANI film in sensors.

The relative amounts of PANI and PTSA are still an important aspect to realize the optimum combination for the best sensor application. The present work is based on the use of different concentrations of PTSA doped PANI in emeraldine salt (PANI) which were systematically prepared to obtain an optimum PTSA concentration for maximum conductivity through the chemical synthesis of PANI. PANI has been investigated for its architecture, optical characteristics, and electrical characteristics resulting from PTSA in the solution. PANI and different concentrations of PTSA doped PANI (PANI-PTSA samples) were characterized using FT-IR, UV–visible (UV–vis), field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), and electrical analysis. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of the PANI and PANI-PTSA samples was perceived by the photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL) method. The PTSA doped PANI in the best PTSA concentration using exceptional preparation, properties and maximum fluorescence intensity for a limit of detection of concentration's pyrene was also estimated.

Aniline (99%), PTSA, Hexane (98%), pyrene, ammonium persulfate (APS 98%), N-Methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP), and H2O2 were delivered using Avanti Chemicals of Sigma Aldrich, Darmstadt, Germany. The Microscope glasses were provided by Jiangsu Huida Medical Instruments Co., Ltd. (Yancheng, Jiangsu, China). All materials are included by analytical grade. Deionized water was similarly applied for washing PANI.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of PANI and different concentrations of PTSA doped PANI
ce:section.ce:para: The PANI in emeraldine salt (PANI) was prepared according to the previous literature [3,37]. 1.12 g of aniline was dissolved in 60 mL of 0.4 M PTSA as an acidic medium, continually stirred for 1 h, chilled and dropwise added with 5 mL of APS (0.34 g of APS dissolved in 5 mL of 0.4 M PTSA). An ice bath was applied to keep the temperature from 0 to 5 ◦C. For an additional 3 h, the combination was stirred. To achieve white filtrate, the sedimented crude polymer is filtered and washed throughout deionized water until it is no longer coloured. The resulting substance was dehydrated for 12 h at 50 °C inside a furnace. After that, for preparing the PANI solution, 0.4320 g of PANI precipitate was dispersed into 14.4 mL of NMP as a common solvent (10.286 mol/L). The combination was got heated at 80 °C for 40 min and stimulated at 350 rpm for 24 h at ambient temperature.

The present study aims at the effect of PANI-PTSA samples, which were prepared by adding PTSA with different wt% (0.5–6 wt %) and stirring at 350 rpm, then sonication for 1 h as shown in Table 1 . To prepare the aqueous solution of PTSA as dopant was maintained throughout the synthesis in all cases (3:1 ratio). Later, PTSA 6% in solution form as a reference of PTSA solution, was achieved by dispersion of 1.80 g of PTSA into 30 mL of distilled water. Then, other PTSA concentrations, i.e., 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5% were obtained by diluting the PTSA 6% solution.

Pyrene solution was prepared as follows; brieﬂy, 0.00250 g of pyrene was dissolved in 250 mL of hexane to achieve 10 ppm of pyrene solution. Other concentrations, i.e., 0.001, 0.01, 0.1, and 1 ppm were obtained by diluting the 10 ppm pyrene solution.

To prepare the thin film of PANI and PANI-PTSA samples, the microscope glasses were first immersed in methanol for 20 min, later dipped in solution covering 4.0 mL H2O2, 4.0 mL NH4OH, and 15.0 mL of distilled water for an extra 15 min. Later, microscopic slides were dehydrated in the oven for 1 h. At various levels, the PANI and PANI-PTSA solutions have been calibrated to 80 μl using the compounds. Finally, using POLOS™ spin coater at 1000 r/m for 20 s, the fine film was placed on a glass surface. Later, the films were kept in a safe place and checked after 24 h. The thickness measurement was also conducted by surface roughness tester (SV-Mitutoyo-3000) using the scratch after deposition method.

FT-IR spectroscopy was performed with KBr pellets (Thermo Nicolet 6700 FT-IR, USA) within a series of 500–4000 cm−1 to classify the surface state and functional groups of the PANI and PANI-PTSA samples. The optical properties were conducted using UV–vis characterization (UV–vis, PerkinElmer, Lambda 35, USA) at room temperature. The morphology patterns were shown by a field emission scanning electron microscope (FEI, NOVA NANOSEM 230, Switzerland). The thickness was measured using a surface roughness tester (SV-Mitutoyo-3000, Switzerland). The four-point probe (Four-terminal sensing) was applied to conduct the electrical conductivity using a 4-terminal probe of the MCCAT (Mitsubishi, Model: Loresta GX (MCP-T700)) Measuring Systems. The fluorescence intensity was verified on the PANI and PANI –PTSA samples films with PL settings (PL, PerkinElmer, and LS 55), while the wavelengths of excitation and emission intensities were reported at 256 and 330 nm, separately.

Fig. 2 illustrates the materialization of PANI and PANI-PTSA samples authenticated via FT-IR characterization in the range of 500–4000 cm−1. PANI and PANI-PTSA samples structures are mostly the same, and the spectra show the following major vibration bands as shown in Table 2 . The absorption peaks at 3422, 3203, and 3121 cm−1 can be assigned to the overlapping of NH characteristic stretching symmetric and asymmetric stretching of OH from SO3H [38,39], while the peaks at 2904 and 2831 cm−1 related to C–H stretching [39].

The most significant bands at 1651, and 1653 cm−1 can be owing to the CN extension of benzenoid and quinoid rings simultaneously [40]. The other important peaks at 1584 cm−1 are because of CC stretching oscillations of the quinoid ring and other peaks at 1501 cm−1 are related to CC stretching of the benzenoid ring [37,39,41,42]. Other peaks at 1321 and 1226 cm−1 are related to the C–N stretching band of the aromatic amine polarized band (N = Q = N) which is representative of the conductivity of PANI due to the charge delocalization on the PANI backbone [37,39,43].

The band observed in the 1099 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching of the SO3 − group [26,27,44]. The existence of the SO3 −1 group is normal in PANI and PANI-PTSA samples. Since PTSA has a dual role in the PANI backbone, the first is an acidic medium and the second is dopant.

The peak at 879, 832 cm−1 represents the C–H bending for the substituted aromatic ring [37,45]. The presence of peaks at 633 and 590 cm−1 can be assigned to the stretching deformation of the aromatic ring and C–S stretching vibrational mode [42].

Throughout the PANI and PANI-PTSA specimens, the extension of the resultant FT-IR bands suggests that the manufacturing in the oxidative chemical polymerization results in bridge development among all specimens [46]. The intensity will be increased by decreasing PTSA concentrations, and results show PANI and PANI-PTSA samples possess an extremely sharp intensity of polarized band which is the measure of the delocalization of the conductivity or electrons [46].

Using the intensity of polarized, benzenoid, and quinoid, it is possible to estimate their relative proportions throughout the surface [47]. They are produced in roughly similar quantities in the polymer because their maxima are nearly identical and their intensities vary owing to variations in the extent of PTSA loading [46]. This may indicate PANI polymerization in an emeraldine salt, which would be interesting to investigate [39]. In this study, the mechanism of PANI and PANI-PTSA samples are similar since PTSA was used as an acidic medium and dopant simultaneously. On the other hand, all the characteristic peaks in PANI are visible in the spectra of PANI-PTSA samples. However, the intensity seems different and it will be increased by decreasing PTSA concentrations [26,27,44]. Comparison is made only to show that preparation of PANI for all samples would have some conductivity value which was further measured by the respective method of the Four-terminal sensing method.

The effect of PTSA concentrations on PANI was examined through UV–vis spectroscopic analysis, as shown in Fig. 3 . From these spectra, it can be quickly determined that PANI has been effectively synthesized in the salt form, allowing easy measurement. Furthermore, when looking at PANI-PTSA specimens, the existence of two peaks throughout the visible area indicates that they were accumulated in the loaded condition [48].

All specimens potentially lead to three distinct absorption bands in the ranges 273–293, 365–385, and 550–800 nm with minor differences throughout band status, which correlate with π–π*, polaron-π*, and π to polaron transformations of the benzenoid, quinoid, and polarized bonds respectively [49–51]. The humps at 327 and 383 nm are recently produced in the PANI-PTSA samples. There is a gradual blue shift of the band from 326 to 330 nm and 383–387 nm with the increase of PTSA concentrations emergence of the new band cannot be definitively explained at this time; however, one possible explanation stems from the charge movement between the donation PANI and the recipient PTSA chains. When the polymer is doped, the UV–vis findings show that a polaronic band is shaped among the band gaps of the polymer. Furthermore, it is found that when the dopant concentration increases, the peak corresponding to the polaron-π*band transition at 365–385 nm becomes more prominent. Generally, the degree of loading and the length of polymeric conjugation are two essential variables contributing to variations in peak locations and intensities throughout the UV–vis spectrum of such specimens [52–54].

When the dopant concentration rises, the number of concentrated gap situations throughout the polaronic band enhances. As a result, this leads to a rise in the intensity because of the rising PTSA concentrations.

The bandgap measurements of PANI and PANI-PTSA samples were obtained using the following Tauc Equation (1) [55,56];

where h is Planck's constant, υ is the frequency of the photon, K is a constant, Eg is the bandgap energy, and α is the absorption coefficient (cm−1), which is calculated from the following Equation (2) [57]:

A is the absorbance, which is calculated from Fig. 3 and t is the thickness of the sample, which is equal to 1 cm.

The band gap can be calculated by extrapolating the straight line at α = 0 from the (αhν)2 versus hν curves are shown in Fig. 4 . The findings of the experiments indicate that the bandgap of PANI changes significantly along with loading. As a result, the band gap increases with the PTSA concentrations from 5.25 to 5.36 eV while the PANI has a high bandgap with a value of about 6.45 eV. Table 3 describes, that by decreasing the bandgap of PANI and PANI-PTSA samples, conductivity will be increased because of the density of electrons.

Fig. 5a-h and 6
a-h illustrate the morphology and layer thicknesses of PANI and PANI-PTSA samples by FE-SEM and the transversal cross-section. The morphology of PANI depends on the method of synthesis, solvent, medium, dopant, and amount of these parameters [58]. Entirely the patterns depict the structure of PANI and PANI-PTSA samples at 10,000 magnification. It is apparent that their composition is closely packaged, and the dimensions and form of individual molecules are hard to determine. As previously observed, PANI in emeraldine form prefers to accumulate during the polymerization mechanism [59,60].

The dramatic change in the surface morphology has been observed with the increasing concentration of PTSA in the structure of PANI. At lower content of PTSA (>2 wt %), the almost nanorods surface is formed when the nanoflakes change to nanorods. PANI-PTSA-2 seems the most nanoflakes morphology in its structure. With (3–5%) of PTSA, hexagonal nanopetals oriented in a random direction are observed. At higher content of PTSA (6 wt %), nanorods changed to nanopetals which glued together. Consequently, during the polymer crystallization, the molecular string will extend and parallel the orientation of stirring and shear strength [61]. The FE-SEM images help us conclude that the doping of PTSA has a strong effect on the PANI morphology. With increasing PTSA content, the structure transforms morphology from typical nanoflakes PANI to nanorods and nanopetals.

In this research, the FE-SEM method traversed PANI, and PANI-PTSA specimens were analyzed to determine the surface widths. The contents of the PANI are condensed, with the sheets resembling interconnected buildings formed only on the lamella borders. The PANI, and PANI-PTSA in concentration as 0.5%, 1%, 2%, 3%,4%, 5% and 6% of PTSA have thickness of about 12.71, 9.32, 8.28, 10.14, 22.85, 23.90, 27.51 and 29.49 μm, respectively. That means by increasing the concentration of PTSA; the thickness will be increased. Due to the increase in the amount of current intensity and the reduction in the spacing between the anode and substrate, it is anticipated that the width will rise dramatically.

The four-probe method determines the conductivity of materials at room temperature. The Four-terminal sensing detected voltage differences when a current was passed from the PANI and PANI-PTSA films. The resistance to current flow inversely showed the electrical conductivity of PANI and PANI-PTSA films.

The PANI's conductivity depends on two parameters; the first is the grade of oxidation, and the second is the grade of protonation [62]. In addition, features of PANI are dependent on dopant type, the concentration of dopant, concentration of solvent, polymerization conditions (temperature, pH, and time), and monomer to oxidant molar ratio [63]. In this research, the electrical properties of PANI and PANI-PTSA samples were measured using the Four-terminal sensing technique.

Resistance (Ω) was employed for this aim following the Four-terminal sensing technique. Nevertheless, the resistance differs following the particles' nature, structure, composition, and measurement position. The authorized measurement technique thus utilizes resistivity (Ω∙cm) that represents exact and actual substance characteristics. The resistance is calculated directly by multiplying the resistance recorded in the resistance correction factor (RCF). In this research, the electrical measurement devices are intended to simplify RCF calculation, therefore the resistance is determined by a straightforward testing technique. Additionally, the dimension of samples reported 7.5 × 15 (mm) in a stable position, which RCF is reported at 8.857.

Conductivity (σ) is in reverse of volume resistivity. It is similarly termed specific conductivity. The unit of conductivity is S/cm or (Ω cm)−1, so by Equation (6), the conductivity (σ) was calculated for PANI samples as given in Table 4 .

In this study, the thickness of layers was measured by FE-SEM as presented in Fig. 6a-h and, electrical data were reported in Table 4, and compared with other research as mentioned in Table 5 .

Conductivity values were recorded when samples were dehydrated at ambient temperature for 48 h. The varying conductivities for PANI and PANI-PTSA samples were achieved. Each concentration leads to a different doping state of polymer structure, while electronic behaviour depends on structural disorder and resonance of charges [64].

The conductivity of PANI and PANI-PTSA samples will be increased in the presence of PTSA as a dopant since PTSA corresponds to enhancing the conductivity of PANI [31]. The results also showed by increasing PTSA concentrations, conductivity will be decreased because of the increased thickness of layers. Another reason can be owing to the maximum density of electrons and the reduction in the PANI size [31]. The damages caused to the structure's PANI in high PTSA concentration [34]. As shown in Fig. 7 , the highest conductivity is reported at 2.7 (Ω cm)−1 of PANI-PTSA-2. This is clear that PANI-PTSA in different PTSA concentrations has higher conductivity than PANI.

Fig. 8 depicts the PL spectrum of the PANI and PANI-PTSA samples taken with emission and excitation wavelengths of 256 and 330 nm, respectively. Fig. 8 (a) shows the excitation curves of PANI and PANI-PTSA samples have typical peaks at around 256. Fig. 8 (b) depicts all the emission intensity of PANI-PTSA samples that have typical peaks at around 330 and 382 nm, which intensifies as the dopant concentration increases; however, the peak of PANI is shown just at 330 nm because of the doping level.

Fig. 3 showed the UV–Vis spectra of PANI-PTSA-2, which, except for PANI-PTSA-2, exhibited a single wide polaronic band at about 385 nm that became more intense with increasing dopant levels. The number of gaps throughout the polaronic band is expanding too, leading to more transitions from the polaronic band to the π-band (HOMO), and hence to an intensification of the PL maximum at 382 nm.

On the other side, the electrons of the valence band are stimulated to the conductance band and subsequently moved into the polymer backbone after the photo excitement of a conjugated polymer. The electron that has been stimulated and the gap that has been charged in the reverse direction are drawn together. Whenever the stimulated electron rejoins the gap, a photon is expelled during a condition termed fluorescence. When stimulated in the visible range, this static connection inhibits the flow of electrons along the conjugated sequence or causes ultra-fast photo-induced electron transport to the recipient, leading to a reduction in the fluorescence intensity of the conjugated polymer as seen in PANI-PTSA specimens.

In any case, is assumed that PANI-PTSA samples emit incoherence luminescence waves in all directions with amplitudes proportional to the amplitude of the exciting wave at the location of the PANI-PTSA specimens. The increasing thickness of PANI-PTSA films shows substantial distortions on the PL spectrum of films, like the presence of shoulders together with two peaks. So by increasing concentrations of PTSA, the thickness of PANI-PTSA films was increased and PL findings indicated the rising in the PL intensity which may be ascribed to the decrease of PANI-PTSA films thickness [67–69].

Fluorescence studies established that PANI-PTSA-2 has a high fluorescence intensity within the PANI-PTSA samples. This outcome might be assigned to the tight organization of benzenoid and quinoid parts that helps excitons form and increase the length of a singlet exciton. The PANI-PTSA-2, with its progressive fluorescence and electrical features, illustrates an exceptional adventure point of gathering electrons for pyrene detection in sensor usage.

Fig. 9 illustrates the fluorescence intensity of PANI-PTSA-2 which interact with pyrene in different concentrations as 0.001, 0.01, 0.1,1 and 10 ppm. Fig. 9(a) depicts the excitation intensity at 254 nm of PANI-PTSA-2 which interacted with different Pyrene concentrations in the range of (0.001–10) ppm. Fig. 9(b) displayed, that the emission intensity appeared at 326 nm of PANI-PTSA-2 when the PANI-PTSA-2 adsorbed the Pyrene concentrations in the range of (0.001–10) ppm. Consequently, the fluorescence intensity decreased by increasing pyrene concentration from 0.001 ppm to 10 ppm [1,70,71].

The equation related to Otto Stern and Max Volmer (Stern–Volmer) was applied to investigate the interface between PANI-PTSA-2 and pyrene Equation (7) as follows:

where F is the fluorescence intensity of PANI-PTSA-2 when interacted pyrene concentrations and F

p is the quencher and Ksv is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant (pyrene) concentration [72].

A PL experiment was done 8 times to investigate the constancy of the fluorescence features of PANI-PTSA-2. Fig. 9(c) presents a good linear correlation between (F
0
/F)-1 and the concentration of pyrene in the range of 0.001–10 ppm with a correlation coefficient of R2 = 0.99.
The standard deviation (σ) and slope of the variation curve are 0.28 and 0.13, separately.

The limit of detection (LOD) is related to the σ and Ksv of variation of fluorescence intensity with pyrene concentrations which are calculated from the following Equation (8);
(8)
L O D = 3.3

As a result, the LOD has achieved about 7 × 10−3 ppm. As revealed in Table 6 , the results of PANI-PTSA-2 as a sensing material for detecting pyrene are compared with that of other published conductive polymer-based, which established high sensitivity and low detection limit of prepared PANI-PTSA-2, indicating the superiority of such conductive polymers.

In summary, PANI and PANI-PTSA samples were prepared using the chemical oxidation method. To comprehend intensity variations in PTSA levels, FT-IR spectroscopy was helpful to evaluate the intensity proportion of quinoid/benzenoid. The UV–vis findings demonstrated by decreasing the bandgap of PANI-PTSA samples, conductivity will be increased because of the density of electrons. The FE-SEM presented the doping of PTSA has a strong effect on the PANI morphology where increasing PTSA content in PANI-PTSA samples caused a transformation from typical nanoflakes to nanorods and nanopetals. The electrical conductivity was decreased with rising the bandgap value and PANI-PTSA-2 showed a low bandgap of 5.23 eV and high conductivity of 2.7 (Ω cm)−1.

Furthermore, the PTSA content has been found to enhance the magnitude of the fluorescence in PANI-PTSA samples and PANI-PTSA-2 showed high PL intensities owing to its orderly organized benzenoid and quinoid parts. In conclusion, PANI-PTSA-2 was selected as appropriate material due to its exceptional preparation, low bandgap, high electrical properties, and high fluorescence intensity to detect the pyrene in the different concentrations using the PL method. The limit of the detection for pyrene was about 7 × 10−3 ppm in a linear range of 0.001–10 ppm. Therefore, PANI-PTSA-2 will be a suitable candidate for researchers and industries to improve sensing materials for detecting of other polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) in complicated sensor setups in future work.
